active_paradigms.idea: Geometric construction with corner-first placement strategy
active_paradigms.description: Build the packing explicitly using geometric principles. Start by placing 4 large circles at corners with radius ≈ 0.207 (touching two walls). Then place 8 circles along edges (one between each corner pair). Fill the remaining 14 interior circles using hexagonal packing pattern. Calculate radii from contact constraints: corner circles touch two walls and potentially one neighbor, edge circles touch one wall and neighbors, interior circles touch neighbors only. This exploits the geometric insight that circles with fewer wall constraints can be larger.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by maximizing individual circle sizes through strategic placement
active_paradigms.cautions: Verify all constraints are satisfied; use geometric calculations for exact contact points; the pattern must fit 26 circles total (4 corners + 8 edges + 14 interior)

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for better constraint handling
active_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with trust-constr which handles nonlinear constraints more robustly. Trust-constr uses a trust-region approach that can better navigate the complex constraint landscape of circle packing. Implement analytical Jacobians for constraints (both boundary and non-overlap) to improve convergence. Use sparse constraint Jacobian since each constraint only involves 2 circles. The method approximates the Hessian of the Lagrangian, which can help escape poor local minima that trap SLSQP.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through improved constrained optimization convergence
active_paradigms.cautions: Trust-constr requires constraints in a different format; implement constraint Jacobians for efficiency; may need more iterations than SLSQP; use 'lanczos' or 'svd' for subspace method

active_paradigms.description: Apply differential_evolution as a global optimizer that doesn't rely on gradients and can escape local minima. This is well-suited for non-convex problems with many constraints. Use the 'best1bin' or 'randtobest1bin' strategy. Wrap constraints using the penalty method or the new constraint interface in scipy. The population-based approach explores the solution space more broadly than gradient-based methods, potentially finding better packings that SLSQP misses.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through global search avoiding local minima traps
active_paradigms.cautions: Differential evolution is slower per iteration; use appropriate population size (15-20 per dimension); set maxiter high enough (1000+); constraints must be handled via penalty or wrapper; bounds are naturally supported

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with SLSQP for joint position-radius optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Treat all 78 variables (x, y, r for each of 26 circles) as continuous optimization variables. Objective: minimize negative sum of radii. Constraints: (1) boundary constraints x_i - r_i >= 0, x_i + r_i <= 1, y_i - r_i >= 0, y_i + r_i <= 1 as inequalities, (2) non-overlap constraints sqrt((x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2) - r_i - r_j >= 0 for all pairs. Use SLSQP method which handles nonlinear constraints. Initialize from current hexagonal pattern. Run multiple optimizations with perturbed starting points (add small random noise to initial positions) to escape local minima. Use 1000-2000 max iterations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary metric: sum of radii (target ratio to 2.635)
tried_paradigms.cautions: Non-overlap constraints are non-convex, so multiple restarts are essential. Use constraint tolerance of 1e-6 to match evaluator. Ensure radii stay positive with bounds. The problem has many local optima, so try at least 10 different initializations.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi for geometric radius maximization
tried_paradigms.description: Exploit Voronoi diagram structure to determine optimal circle positions. Initialize points using current pattern. Compute Voronoi diagram with scipy.spatial.Voronoi. For each point, the maximum inscribed circle radius equals the minimum distance to: (1) Voronoi cell edges (determined by neighboring points), and (2) square boundaries. This gives exact upper bounds on radii. Then optimize point positions to maximize sum of these radii: for each point, move it toward the centroid of its Voronoi cell to increase minimum distance to edges. Iterate Voronoi computation and point movement until convergence. Use scipy.optimize.minimize on point positions only (52 variables) with radii computed analytically from Voronoi structure.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Sum of radii computed from Voronoi cell geometry
tried_paradigms.cautions: Handle boundary points carefully - their Voronoi cells extend outside the square. For points near edges, clip Voronoi cells to the unit square. Use scipy.spatial.distance.cdist for efficient distance computations. Ridge points and ridge vertices from Voronoi need careful indexing.

tried_paradigms.idea: Physics-based iterative expansion without optimization library
tried_paradigms.description: Use a simulation approach where circles expand and push each other apart. Start with small circles (r=0.01) at good initial positions. Iteratively: (1) Expand all radii by small factor (1.01x), (2) For each overlapping pair, move circles apart along the line connecting their centers by half the overlap distance, (3) For circles outside boundaries, project them back inside and reduce their radius to fit, (4) Repeat until no more expansion possible. This mimics physical expansion of gas bubbles and finds locally optimal packings without formal optimization. Use numpy for efficient vectorized distance computations. Run multiple simulations with different initial arrangements.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Final sum of radii after simulation converges
tried_paradigms.cautions: Use small expansion factors (1.001-1.01) for stability. Limit iterations to prevent infinite loops. Handle degenerate cases where circles have zero valid radius. Use numpy vectorization for O(n^2) distance computations efficiently. Multiple random initializations needed since this is a local method.
